title: Distinguishing Bohmian contextuality from Kochen-Specker contextuality
abstract: Quantum contextuality is a concept used to describe the property of hidden-variable theory that measurement outcomes predetermined by the hidden variables depend on the measurement context. The term measurement context can have different meanings, giving rise to different flavours of quantum contextuality. The first discovered flavour is Kochen-Specker (KS) contextuality where measurement outcomes will depend on what compatible measurements are jointly performed with the selected measurement. Another flavour, here to be compared with KS contextuality, is that referred to in Bohmian mechanics where outcomes of some specific measurements are not completely specified by the model state, but depend also on specifics of the measurement device used. It has been claimed that this type of Bohmian contextuality is necessary to enable KS contextuality in a hidden variable model. In this paper we show that this is not the case. The recently proposed Contextual Ontological Model (COM) [Hindlycke and Larsson, Phys. Rev. Lett. 2022] produces KS contextual predictions but does not have the Bohmian contextuality; the outcome of every measurement allowed by COM can be predicted from the model state itself. This distinguishes Bohmian contextuality from KS contextuality, and enables individual study of the two concepts.
summary: This paper proves that the measurement-device dependence characteristic of Bohmian mechanics is not a prerequisite for Kochen-Specker contextuality. By using the Contextual Ontological Model, the authors show that a model can exhibit KS contextuality while having outcomes predictable solely from the model state. This allows for the independent study of these two distinct forms of quantum contextuality.
main_problem: The paper aims to clarify the relationship between two types of quantum contextuality: Kochen-Specker (KS) contextuality and Bohmian contextuality, specifically testing if Bohmian-type measurement dependence is a necessary requirement for KS contextuality.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that Bohmian contextuality is not a necessary condition for KS contextuality, as the Contextual Ontological Model (COM) reproduces KS predictions without requiring measurement-device dependence.
method: The study uses a comparative analysis between Bohmian mechanics and the Contextual Ontological Model (COM) using a spin-1/2 system and simulated Stern-Gerlach measurement setups.
model_or_system: The analysis focuses on a spin-1/2 particle system using Bohmian mechanics (trajectory-based) and the Contextual Ontological Model (an extension of stabilizer quantum mechanics) within a Stern-Gerlach apparatus framework.
key_observables: Particle position, spin states (up/down), and measurement outcomes (exit paths in a Stern-Gerlach device).
important_parameters: Magnetic pole polarity (SGS vs. SGN devices), particle velocity, and the Quantum Equilibrium Hypothesis.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes simplified, non-spreading rectangular wave packets, neglects y-direction dynamics, and is restricted to the framework of stabilizer quantum mechanics for the COM model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Stern-Gerlach device scenarios for different spin/polarity combinations; Figure 2 illustrates Bohmian trajectories splitting at a threshold; Figure 3 depicts a three-stage measurement setup to demonstrate spin assignment dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the two types of contextuality, defines the Bohmian and COM models, performs a comparative analysis of spin assignment in Stern-Gerlach setups, and concludes by showing the independence of the two concepts.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
